title: Plastics
items: Water bottles, milk tea cups, clean food containers.
description: Only clean, rigid plastics belong here. If the item holds its shape (like a bottle or a cup), drop it in. Make sure to empty all liquids and food residue first.
note: Note: Soft, crinkly plastics like wrappers and sachets go in the Non-Biodegradable (Black) bin.

itemsList.name: Plastic Water Bottle
itemsList.description: Clear plastic bottles (PET) are highly recyclable. Please prep them before throwing them away to maximize bin space!
prepNotes: Empty: Pour out any leftover water (liquids ruin other recyclables!).
Crush: Twist or flatten the bottle to save space.
Cap: Screw the cap back on to keep the bottle crushed.

itemsList.name: Plastic Soda Bottle
itemsList.description: Just like water bottles, plastic soda bottles are highly valuable for recycling. Because soda is sticky, a little extra care is needed.
prepNotes: Empty: Pour out any leftover soda completely.
Rinse: Give it a quick swirl with water to remove the sticky syrup.
Crush & Cap: Squeeze the air out to flatten the bottle, then screw the cap back on.

itemsList.name: Milk Tea Cup
itemsList.description: Thick plastic cups used for milk tea are recyclable, but the sugary residue and plastic sealing film can contaminate the recycling batch.
prepNotes: Empty: Pour out all leftover tea, ice, and tapioca pearls.
Remove Film: Peel off the soft plastic sealing film (this goes in the Non-Biodegradable bin).
Rinse: Give the cup a quick rinse.

itemsList.name: Plastic Spoon & Fork
itemsList.description: Rigid plastic cutlery can be recycled, but they must be completely free of food residue.
prepNotes: Wipe or Rinse: Wash off all oils, sauces, and food bits.
Dry: Shake off excess water before tossing them in the bin.

itemsList.name: Clean Food Tupperware
itemsList.description: Heavy-duty microwaveable plastic tubs (like the ones from takeout restaurants) are excellent for recycling, provided they are not greasy.
prepNotes: Wash: Thoroughly wash with soap and water to remove all oil. Oil ruins the plastic recycling process.
Separate: Detach the lid from the base before throwing them in.

title: Paper
description: Clean, dry paper products belong here. Paper fibers are highly recyclable as long as they are not ruined by food grease or moisture.
note: Note: Wet paper or heavily greasy boxes (like pizza boxes) go to Residual Waste.

itemsList.name: Cardboard Boxes
itemsList.description: Corrugated cardboard boxes from deliveries or appliances are highly valuable.
prepNotes: Flatten: Break down the box completely to save space.
Keep Dry: Wet or greasy cardboard cannot be recycled and goes to Residual.

itemsList.name: Scratch & Bond Paper
itemsList.description: Used printer paper, loose-leaf bond paper, and notebook pages.
prepNotes: Remove Metal: Take out heavy binder clips or staples if possible.
Keep Dry: Paper must be completely dry.

itemsList.name: Newspaper
itemsList.description: Standard daily newspapers and broadsheets are fully recyclable.
prepNotes: Stack: Tie them in neat bundles if disposing of a large amount so they don't blow away.

itemsList.name: Glossy Magazines
itemsList.description: Glossy magazines, catalogs, and brochures are recyclable as long as they are clean.
prepNotes: Keep Dry: Ensure they are not wet or soiled.

itemsList.name: Paper Bags
itemsList.description: Standard brown or white paper grocery bags.
prepNotes: Fold: Fold them flat to save space in the bin.
Check for Grease: If the bottom is soaked in fast-food oil, it goes to Residual.

itemsList.name: Thermal Receipts
itemsList.description: Store and ATM receipts are printed on thermal paper, which contains chemicals (like BPA) that contaminate paper recycling.
prepNotes: Throw in Residual: Do NOT put these in the recyclable bin. They go straight to Non-Biodegradable/Residual waste.

title: Cans & Glass
items: Soda cans, glass bottles, metal caps.
description: Metal cans and glass containers. These materials are incredibly valuable because they can be melted down and recycled infinitely without losing quality.
note: Note: Broken glass should be wrapped in thick newspaper and labeled before disposal to protect waste collectors.

itemsList.name: Aluminum Soda Cans
itemsList.description: Aluminum is infinitely recyclable and one of the most valuable materials in the bin.
prepNotes: Empty: Pour out remaining liquids.
Crush: Step on the can to flatten it and save space.

itemsList.name: Glass Bottles
itemsList.description: Glass bottles (like beer, wine, or soy sauce bottles) are fully recyclable but must be handled carefully.
prepNotes: Rinse: Wash out sticky residue.
Be Careful: Place gently in the bin so it does not shatter.

itemsList.name: Tin Food Cans
itemsList.description: Steel and tin cans used for sardines, corned beef, and evaporated milk.
prepNotes: Rinse: Wash out leftover oils and food to prevent pests.
Push Lid: If the sharp metal lid is still attached, push it inside the can so nobody gets cut.

itemsList.name: Metal Bottle Caps
itemsList.description: Small metal crowns from glass soda or beer bottles.
prepNotes: Collect: Toss them loosely into the cans/glass bin.

title: Food Waste
items: Leftover rice, fruit peels, coffee grounds.
description: Organic matter that will decompose naturally. This includes food scraps, yard trimmings, and compostable materials.
note: Note: Avoid pouring large amounts of cooking oil or soup broth directly into the bin.

itemsList.name: Leftover Rice
itemsList.description: Spoiled or leftover rice is fully compostable.
prepNotes: Drain Fluids: Do not dump oily broths or soups directly into the bin.
Separate: Ensure no plastic spoons or takeout container pieces are mixed in.

itemsList.name: Bones & Meat
itemsList.description: Leftover fried chicken bones, pork ribs, and meat trimmings belong in the biodegradable bin.
prepNotes: Wrap if Odorous: If your local collection takes a few days, wrap meat scraps in old newspaper to minimize flies.

itemsList.name: Fruit Peels
itemsList.description: Banana peels, mango skins, and apple cores break down naturally and are perfect for compost.
prepNotes: Remove Stickers: Peel off those tiny plastic fruit stickers first (they go to Residual)!

itemsList.name: Coconut Shells
itemsList.description: Harder organic materials like coconut shells and copra scraps.
prepNotes: Chop: For large items like coconut husks, chopping them speeds up decomposition if composting at home.

itemsList.name: Used Coffee Grounds
itemsList.description: Used coffee grounds are excellent for compost and garden soil.
prepNotes: Cool Down: Let hot coffee grounds cool before throwing them away.

itemsList.name: Used Tea Bags
itemsList.description: Paper tea bags will decompose naturally.
prepNotes: Check for Plastic: Some premium pyramid tea bags are made of plastic mesh. If it feels like plastic, it goes to Residual waste.

title: E-Waste
items: Dead batteries, broken wires, light bulbs.
description: Electronic devices, batteries, and appliances. These contain toxic materials and heavy metals that require special handling.
note: Note: Do not mix e-waste with regular trash to prevent chemical leaks into the soil.

itemsList.name: Household Batteries
itemsList.description: Dead AA, AAA, and button cell batteries contain toxic chemicals and must NEVER be thrown in regular trash.
prepNotes: Store Safely: Keep them in a dry, sealed container until you can drop them at a local e-waste collection point.

itemsList.name: Broken Cables & Wires
itemsList.description: Old phone chargers, earphones, and extension cords contain valuable copper.
prepNotes: Tie them up: Coil them neatly so they don't tangle with other e-waste.

itemsList.name: Burnt Light Bulbs
itemsList.description: LED and fluorescent tubes contain trace amounts of hazardous materials.
prepNotes: Handle with Care: Wrap in old newspaper or its original box to prevent shattering during transport.

itemsList.name: Broken Small Electronics
itemsList.description: Calculators, old computer mice, broken cellphones, and power banks.
prepNotes: Wipe Data: If disposing of an old phone, try to factory reset it first if it still turns on.

title: Wrappers
items: Junk food bags, sachets, dirty styrofoam, foil.
description: Composite materials, soft plastics, and heavily soiled items that cannot be recycled or composted.
note: Note: Always empty liquids from sachets to prevent your trash bin from smelling bad.

itemsList.name: Fast Food Burger Wrappers
itemsList.description: Paper wrappers lined with foil or thin plastic cannot be recycled because of the grease and mixed materials.
prepNotes: Scrape: Remove leftover food scraps (throw those in Food Waste) before tossing the wrapper.

itemsList.name: Condiment & Sauce Sachets
itemsList.description: Small tear-open packets for ketchup, gravy, or soy sauce are residual waste. The same applies to single-use shampoo or coffee sachets.
prepNotes: Empty Fully: Squeeze out as much of the liquid as possible to prevent a sticky mess.

itemsList.name: Junk Food Bags
itemsList.description: Snack bags and candy wrappers have a shiny metallic interior mixed with plastic. This composite material goes straight to residual waste.
prepNotes: Flatten: Roll or fold the empty bag tightly to save space.

itemsList.name: Dirty Styrofoam
itemsList.description: Styrofoam food containers are extremely difficult to recycle and are generally treated as residual waste.
prepNotes: Empty: Dump out leftover food and soup into the compost/sink before throwing the styro away.

itemsList.name: Used Aluminum Foil
itemsList.description: While clean aluminum cans are recyclable, heavily soiled aluminum foil used for cooking or wrapping oily food usually goes to residual waste.
prepNotes: Crush: Crumple it into a ball to save space.
